EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Providence at 340 PM EDT, a tree was split in half on North Main Street; at 410 PM EDT a tree was down on Charles Street; at 426 PM EDT, a tree and wires were down on South Court Street, damaging a house; at 503 PM EDT, wires were down on Modena Avenue.  In North Providence at 425 PM EDT, a trained spotter reported a wind gust to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In West Warwick at 447 PM EDT, a report was received from the public of a 'roof collapse' of an unspecified building. In Warwick at 457 PM EDT, amateur radio operators reported numerous trees and wires down on Strawberry Field Road.  In Warwick at 430 and 436 PM EDT, the ASOS at T.F. Green Airport recorded a wind gust to 59 mph.  Also in Warwick at 543 PM EDT, trees and wires were down on Wayland Avenue and at 553 PM EDT a large tree was down on Julian Road and Church Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cumberland at 355 PM EDT, trees were down on Abbott Run Valley Road and at 430 PM a tree was down on power lines on Massachusetts Avenue.  In Scituate at 415 PM EDT, a tree was down on Central Avenue.  In Glocester at 428 PM, tree was down on Winsor Road.  In Burrillville at 445 PM EDT, a tree was down on Cherry Farm Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated instability combined with an upper level short wave trough to produce scattered severe thunderstorms with large hail. They occurred during the mid evening hours across northernmost Rhode Island and portions of mainly central and eastern Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported in Woonsocket at 820 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in the Gulf of Maine was exiting the region, but there was a strong northwest flow of air in its wake.  Winds gusted to 45 to 55 mph across the region, causing scattered areas of downed trees, some which fell onto cars and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 934 AM EST (1034 AM EDT), the ASOS at T.F. Green Airport, located in Warwick, recorded a wind gust to 48 mph.  In Warwick at 356 PM EST, a tree and power lines were down on Bald Hill Road.  In East Greenwich at 417 PM EST, a tree and wires were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in the Gulf of Maine was exiting the region, but there was a strong northwest flow of air in its wake.  Winds gusted to 45 to 55 mph across the region, causing scattered areas of downed trees, some which fell onto cars and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 934 AM EST (1034 AM EDT), winds gusted to 48 mph at the ASOS at T.F. Green Airport, located in Kent County...but that was representative of winds throughout the state.  In Smithfield at 130 PM EST, a tree and wires were down on Douglas Pike.  In Scituate at 350 PM EST, a tree and power lines were down on Howard Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in the Gulf of Maine was exiting the region, but there was a strong northwest flow of air in its wake.  Winds gusted to 45 to 55 mph across the region, causing scattered areas of downed trees, some which fell onto cars and homes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 934 AM EST (1034 AM EDT), the ASOS at T.F. Green Airport, located in Kent County, recorded a wind gust to 48 mph, but this was representative of winds throughout the state.  In Providence at 1010 AM EST, a tree and wires were down on South Lane and at 1250 PM EST, a tree and power lines were down onto two cars on Horton Street.  No injuries were reported.  Also in Providence, at 421 PM EST, a tree was down on Arlington Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes and brought strong |and damaging winds to Rhode Island Monday late morning through the evening.  A very strong and highly anomalous low level jet brought southerly wind gusts of 60-70 mph ahead of a cold front that moved through Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Sakonnet Vineyards in Little Compton a WeatherFlow station reported at wind gust of 58 mph at 358 PM EST (458 PM EDT).

A very strong and highly anomalous low level jet brought southerly wind gusts of 60-70 mph ahead of a cold front that moved through Monday evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure system tracked across the Great Lakes and brought strong |and damaging winds to Rhode Island Monday late morning through the evening.  A very strong and highly anomalous low level jet brought southerly wind gusts of 60-70 mph ahead of a cold front that moved through Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burrillville at 1044 AM EST (1144 AM EDT) amateur radio reported a tree down on wires on Victory Hwy. Later at 249 PM EST an NWS employee also reported a tree down on a house on Steere Farm Rd. In Foster at 120 PM EST a tree was down blocking South Killingly Rd. In Scituate there was a tree down on a house on Old Plainfield Pike at 220 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked just south of southern New England on the 18th. It brought rain that changed over to a wet snow overnight on the 17th into the 18th that clung to newly-leaved trees.  Only 3 to locally 6 inches of snow fell, highest in the higher elevations of northeast CT, northwest RI, and interior MA. However, it was enough to cause some tree damage across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An average of 5 to 5.5 inches of snow fell in northwest Providence County, with the highest reports being 5.8 inches in Burrillville (public), 5.5 inches in Pascoag (amateur radio), and 5.4 inches in North Foster (Co-Op observer).  However only 2.5 inches was observed in Harrisville (amateur radio) and 4.2 inches in Glocester (trained spotter).  In Glocester at 515 AM EST (615 AM EDT), a medium sized tree was reported down on wires on Money Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved into interior New England on the 16th spread|a warm front early in the morning across northern Massachusetts,|with wintry precipitation transitioning to rain. A strong cold|front then ushered in windy and much colder weather behind it which caused some minor damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio operator reported a large branch down blocking Phillips Hill Rd in Coventry.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Bristol at 635 PM EDT, a 60-foot tree was down on wires on Opechee Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave trough combined with an approaching cold front and a warm, humid environment to produce severe thunderstorms and flash flooding across MA and northern RI during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Armistice Blvd. was flooded and impassable in front of the DPW building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into a warm, humid air mass and produced a round of severe thunderstorms in the afternoon across Massachusetts, northeast Connecticut, and northern Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cumberland, a tree was down on a house...street not documented.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure in New York state moved north into eastern Canada. It was followed by a cold front that moved east across southern New England. The post-frontal tight pressure gradient caused strong southwest to west winds that gusted to 40 to 60 mph and caused some damage across Massachusetts and far northern Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts ranged from 40 to 50 mph across northern Rhode Island, averaging about 45 mph.  In Scituate at 330 PM EST, two trees were down on Route 116.  One of the trees fell on a Providence water truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful low pressure moved from eastern Pennsylvania across central Connecticut and central Massachusetts on February 7, 2020.  A southerly jet of 85 to 95 mph was just above the surface at 925 mb.  Damaging winds occurred across much of eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island with some damage reported farther to the west.|There were widespread power outages and numerous trees down, some falling on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at T. F. Green Airport recorded a wind gust to 60 mph at 152 PM EST.  In Warwick... at 202 PM EST, a tree was down on Spofford Avenue; at 307 PM EST, a large tree was down on a house on Hawksley Avenue; at 436 PM EST, a tree and wires were down on Webb Avenue; and at 549 PM EST, a tree was down on a house on George Arden Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful low pressure moved from eastern Pennsylvania across central Connecticut and central Massachusetts on February 7, 2020.  A southerly jet of 85 to 95 mph was just above the surface at 925 mb.  Damaging winds occurred across much of eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island with some damage reported farther to the west.|There were widespread power outages and numerous trees down, some falling on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Little Compton, the mesonet site at Sakonnet Vineyards measured a gust to 74 mph at 333 PM EST.  At 302 PM EST, a mesonet site at Newport recorded a gust to 62 mph.  Earlier, at 238 PM EST, the ASOS at Newport Airport recorded a gust to 53 mph.  In Jamestown at approximately 3 PM EST, the media reported that a copper roof was blown off of a signal building next to Beavertail Lighthouse.  In Newport between 330 PM and 533 PM EST, multiple trees were reported down across town.  In Middletown at 426 PM EST, a large tree was down on Boulevard East.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Powerful low pressure moved from eastern Pennsylvania across central Connecticut and central Massachusetts on February 7, 2020.  A southerly jet of 85 to 95 mph was just above the surface at 925 mb.  Damaging winds occurred across much of eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island with some damage reported farther to the west.|There were widespread power outages and numerous trees down, some falling on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 3 PM EST, the CWOP station at Charlestown recorded a gust to 69 mph.  At 251 PM EST, the Block Island Jetty mesonet site recorded a gust to 68 mph.  At 248 PM EST, the AWOS at Block Island Airport (KBID) recorded a gust to 60 mph.  Gusts to 54 mph, just below high wind criteria, were recorded a the University of Rhode Island mesonet site at 318 PM EST and at the Westerly Airport (KWST) ASOS at 316 PM EST.  In Narragansett at 617 PM EST, a tree and wires were reported down on Osceola Avenue. Charlestown Police said that they had trees down blocking roads all over the place.  And in South Kingstown, police reported multiple roads impassable.

Damaging winds occurred across much of eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island with some damage reported farther to the west.|There were widespread power outages and numerous trees down, some falling on homes and vehicles.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Cumberland at 345 PM EST, a NWS employee reported a large tree down.  Based on surrounding data, winds were estimated to have gusted to around 55 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Central Falls, the Fire Department reported that at 530 PM EST a tree fell on Perry Street, injuring one person.  The Providence Journal reported that a tree smashed a parked car on Washington Street across from the Providence Public Safety Complex.  Between 3 PM and 4 PM, there were several measured gusts between 54 mph and 56 mph across the city of Providence, as well as in Smithfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported significant street flooding near the Claiborne Pell Newport Bridge. Water was up to the wheel wells of cars and had stranded several vehicles.  Amateur radio operators also reported 18 to 24 inches of flooding on several streets and the Newport rotary, making roads impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported in South Kingstown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Narragansett, a large tree was down on Windermere Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warwick, large branches were down on Williamsburg Drive.  Wires were down at the intersection of Endicott Drive and Strawberry Field Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On Washington Street near Newport Harbor, a large tree was down. A small sailboat was blown over in the harbor.  Large branches were down, with power outages, on both Bellevue Avenue and Easton's Beach.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Narragansett, 12 to 18 inches of street flooding was reported on Beach Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A negatively tilted trough with anomalously cold air aloft moved into southern New England, producing severe thunderstorms and localized flooding during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Newport, there was up to 1 foot of flood waters on several roads.  The Newport Public Library was closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon and early evening, a surface trough dropped southward from New Hampshire and caused severe thunderstorms in central and eastern Massachusetts and northern Rhode Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Coventry, trees fell onto Hill Farm Camp Road, crushing two vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a fine line of low-topped convection that moved across the region from west to east during the morning.  A southerly low level jet of 80 knots at 850 mb, combined with fully leafed trees, helped cause damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP station in Bristol measured a wind gust to 44 mph at 608 AM EDT, but at nearby T.F. Green Airport (KPVD), the ASOS measured a gust to 56 mph at 723 AM EDT.  In Barrington at 433 AM EDT, power lines were down on Adams Point Road and at 627 AM EDT, a tree and wires were down on Bay Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a fine line of low-topped convection that moved across the region from west to east during the morning.  A southerly low level jet of 80 knots at 850 mb, combined with fully leafed trees, helped cause damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gust reports included: North Providence 51 mph at 558 AM EDT (amateur radio) and Providence 47 mph at 736 AM EDT (HADS).  However, at T.F. Green Airport (KPVD) just south of Providence the ASOS recorded a gust to 56 mph at 723 AM EDT.  In Cranston at 630 AM EDT, a tree and wires were down on Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a fine line of low-topped convection that moved across the region from west to east during the morning.  A southerly low level jet of 80 knots at 850 mb, combined with fully leafed trees, helped cause damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet site at Point Judith recorded a wind gust to 61 mph at 707 AM EDT.  Earlier, at 546 AM EDT, a CWOP station at New Shoreham (Block Island) measured a gust to 59 mph.  Other gusts included 49 mph at a CWOP site in Charlestown, 47 mph at a HADS site near South Kingstown, and a 46 mph gust on the ASOS at the Block Island Airport (KBID).  This office did not receive reports of damage in Washington County, but there likely was some tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front produced a fine line of low-topped convection that moved across the region from west to east during the morning.  A southerly low level jet of 80 knots at 850 mb, combined with fully leafed trees, helped cause damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warwick, at T.F. Green Airport (KPVD), the ASOS recorded a wind gust to 56 mph at 723 AM EDT.  Also in Warwick, at 751 AM EDT, a tree was blown down onto I-95, north of the I-95 connector.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow, strong to damaging winds, and minor coastal flooding in southern New England.  This was a tough storm to forecast.  Although it was not deep low pressure and although it passed just south of the 40N/70W benchmark, its precipitation shield was expansive because of a broad 700 mb low over southeast New England, abundant moisture, and a very cold air mass in place.  By far, the heaviest snow fell to the north (Laconia, NH) and west (Binghamton, NY), where more than 3 feet fell.  But amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches across much of southern New England.  There was some minor coastal flooding at the time of high tide and also a flash freeze in eastern Massachusetts as the storm pulled away to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall generally ranged from 6 to 14.3 inches. Some specific amounts included 14.3 in Tiverton, 8.0 inches in Little Compton, 7.0 inches in Middletown, and 6.5 inches in Portsmouth. Wind were gusting to 40 to 45 mph. The ASOS at Newport Airport (KUUU) reported a gust to 43 mph at 3:53 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a cold front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds blew across the region, aided by mixing due to a very cold pool of air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At T.F. Green Airport in Warwick, the ASOS recorded a gust to 51 mph at 858 AM EST.  In Warwick at 940 AM EST, a tree was down in Goddard Memorial State Park.  Also in Warwick, at 1023 AM EST, a tree and wires were down on River Vue Avenue.  In East Greenwich at 1035 AM EST, a tree was down on Division Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passed well to the west of southern New England, with strong to damaging southerly winds across our region.  In addition, 2.00 to 3.75 inches of rain fell across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In North Smithfield at 324 PM EST, amateur radio reported a wind gust to 61 mph.  In Smithfield at 325 PM EST, a tree was down on Log Road.  In Cumberland at 415 PM EST, a NWS employee reported a tree down on Pennsylvania Avenue.  Also in Cumberland at 527 PM EST, an amateur radio operator reported a large tree down on wires on Reservoir Road at Kern Blvd, which is on the east side of the Diamond Hill Reservoir.  In Burrillville at 654 PM EST, a tree was down on Wallum Lake Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passed well to the west of southern New England, with strong to damaging southerly winds across our region.  In addition, 2.00 to 3.75 inches of rain fell across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were generally gusting to 60 mph.  At 251 PM EST, the ASOS at T.F. Green Airport (KPVD) in Warwick recorded a gust to 58 mph and at 324 PM EST, an amateur radio operator reported a gust to 61 mph in North Smithfield.  In Coventry at 724 PM EST, a tree was down on Allison Avenue.

Although it was not deep low pressure and although it passed just south of the 40N/70W benchmark, its precipitation shield was expansive because of a broad 700 mb low over southeast New England, abundant moisture, and a very cold air mass in place.  By far, the heaviest snow fell to the north (Laconia, NH) and west (Binghamton, NY), where more than 3 feet fell.  But amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches across much of southern New England. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow ranged from 5 to 10 inches in the eastern part of Washington County to 8 to 12 inches in the western part. Some of the higher totals included 11.9 inches in Charlestown, 11.5 in Richmond, 10 inches in Narragansett, 9.2 inches in North Kingstown. Winds gusted generally to 45 to 50 mph. The AWOS at the Quonset Airport (KOQU) recorded a gust to 49 mph at 1:50 PM EST on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An anomalously deep, full-latitude mid-level trough over the Mississippi Valley caused a strong frontal system to move up the Appalachians. It brought strong to damaging winds, heavy rain with minor flooding, and well above normal temperatures to southern New England early on Christmas Day.  Winds generally gusted to 40 to 60 mph, except 65 to 70 mph along the southeast Massachusetts coast.  South winds were blowing more than 100 mph only 2000 feet above the ground in eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, but despite temperatures in the lower 60s there, a surface inversion was strong enough to prevent these very damaging winds from reaching the surface.  Two to four inches of rain fell across the region, with the highest totals from central Rhode Island northwestward across northern Connecticut and portions of western and central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warwick, I-95 was flooded at the exit for Jefferson Blvd (Exit 15).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Providence, a car was stuck on Wampanoag Trail (Route 114) due to street flooding.

It brought strong to damaging winds, heavy rain with minor flooding, and well above normal temperatures to southern New England early on Christmas Day.  Winds generally gusted to 40 to 60 mph, except 65 to 70 mph along the southeast Massachusetts coast.  South winds were blowing more than 100 mph only 2000 feet above the ground in eastern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, but despite temperatures in the lower 60s there, a surface inversion was strong enough to prevent these very damaging winds from reaching the surface.  Two to four inches of rain fell across the region, with the highest totals from central Rhode Island northwestward across northern Connecticut and portions of western and central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In New Shoreham (Block Island) at 740 AM EST, a mesonet site recorded a gust to 64 mph.

Two to four inches of rain fell across the region, with the highest totals from central Rhode Island northwestward across northern Connecticut and portions of western and central Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pawtucket at 815 AM EST, a large tree was down on Pawtucket Avenue.

Winds generally gusted to 40 to 60 mph, except 65 to 70 mph along the southeast Massachusetts coast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally were gusting to 50 to 55 mph.  In Charlestown at 933 AM EST, a CWOP station recorded a wind gust to 46 mph.  A gust to 64 mph was recorded farther south on Block Island at 740 AM EST.  In Exeter at 910 AM EST, a tree was down on Route 102 (Ten Rod Road) near the country club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly intensifying storm system produced heavy rain (2-3 inches) which changed to a period of light snow. Strong winds gusted to 40-50 mph. The low pressure system crossed between Nantucket and Cape Cod before lifting northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were generally gusting 40-45 mph. In Cumberland at 3:24 PM EST a large tree branch fell into a house on Farm Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system produced heavy snow, strong to damaging winds, and minor coastal flooding in southern New England.  This was a tough storm to forecast.  Although it was not deep low pressure and although it passed just south of the 40N/70W benchmark, its precipitation shield was expansive because of a broad 700 mb low over southeast New England, abundant moisture, and a very cold air mass in place.  By far, the heaviest snow fell to the north (Laconia, NH) and west (Binghamton, NY), where more than 3 feet fell.  But amounts ranged from 10 to 20 inches across much of southern New England.  There was some minor coastal flooding at the time of high tide and also a flash freeze in eastern Massachusetts as the storm pulled away to the northeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall ranged from 7.5 to 11.9 inches in eastern Kent County. Some specific totals included 11.9 inches in West Warwick, 8.5 inches in East Greenwich. At T.F Green Airport in Warwick (KPVD) 7.9 inches of snow was measured and a wind gust to 44 mph was recorded on the ASOS at 12:51 PM EST on the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system passed well to the west of southern New England on November 30th and early on December 1st.  Strong to damaging wind gusts occurred on November 30th and the entire state of Rhode Island received between 2.20 and 3.78 inches of rain. This led to street flooding, just after midnight on December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warwick, there was two feet of street flooding on Jefferson Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system passed well to the west of southern New England on November 30th and early on December 1st.  Strong to damaging wind gusts occurred on November 30th and the entire state of Rhode Island received between 2.20 and 3.78 inches of rain. This led to street flooding, just after midnight on December 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In East Providence, street flooding was reported on Catamore Boulevard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusted to 45 to 50 mph during the afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front, causing scattered tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds were generally gusting to 40 to 45 mph.  In North Smithfield at 1052 AM EDT, a tree and wires were down on Old Greenville Road between Hanton Road and Deerfield Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest winds gusted to 45 to 50 mph during the afternoon ahead of an approaching cold front, causing scattered tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds generally were gusting to between 40 and 45 mph.  In nearby North Kingstown at 1050 AM EDT, the AWOS at the Quonset Airport (KOQU) recorded a gust to 40 mph.  In Barrington at 150 PM EDT, a tree was down on Nayatt Road at the Rhode Island Country Club.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately after winds and rainfall associated with post-tropical cyclone Zeta (which passed well to our southeast) diminished, a new system approached. This was a rapidly moving upper level low and its associated upper jet max, which moved northeastward from the Mississippi Valley and low pressure again passed to the south of New England. Cold air was streaming into the region from the north. The result was some heavy, wet snow across much of the region, with northern RI receiving 3 to 6 inches. The weight of the snow caused scattered tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall averaged about 5.5 inches across northwest Providence County.  In Burrillville, an amateur radio operator reported 6.0 inches. A CoOp Observer in North Foster recorded 5.7 inches. In Cumberland, a trained spotter reported 5.5 inches.  In Smithfield and North Smithfield, amounts ranged from 4.2 to 4.8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Immediately after winds and rainfall associated with post-tropical cyclone Zeta (which passed well to our southeast) diminished, a new system approached. This was a rapidly moving upper level low and its associated upper jet max, which moved northeastward from the Mississippi Valley and low pressure again passed to the south of New England. Cold air was streaming into the region from the north. The result was some heavy, wet snow across much of the region, with northern RI receiving 3 to 6 inches. The weight of the snow caused scattered tree and power line damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In eastern Kent County, snowfall ranged from 1.6 inches at T.F. Green Airport in Warwick to 3.0 inches in West Warwick.  Its consistency was quite wet and in Warwick at 1 PM EDT, there was a report of a tree down on Milton Road at Post Road due to the weight of the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line formed in New York State then raced eastward in the late afternoon and early evening, gathering strength as it raced across Massachusetts, northeast Connecticut, and northern Rhode Island. Hundreds of thousands of people were left without power in southern New England, as there was widespread tree and power line damage from winds generally gusting to between 50 and 80 mph.  The Storm Prediction Center officially classified it as a derecho.  Prior to the derecho, strong southwest winds gusted to 40-50 mph in the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon, prior to the squall line, southwest winds were gusting to 40 to 50 mph.  In Scituate at 215 PM EDT, the media reported that a tree landed on an occupied car outside of Scituate Middle/High School. Thankfully, the driver was reportedly okay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line formed in New York State then raced eastward in the late afternoon and early evening, gathering strength as it raced across Massachusetts, northeast Connecticut, and northern Rhode Island. Hundreds of thousands of people were left without power in southern New England, as there was widespread tree and power line damage from winds generally gusting to between 50 and 80 mph.  The Storm Prediction Center officially classified it as a derecho.  Prior to the derecho, strong southwest winds gusted to 40-50 mph in the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burrillville, a tree was down on two cars, which were heavily damaged. There were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the wake of a cold front, strong to damaging west to northwest winds blew across the region, aided by mixing due to a very cold pool of air aloft.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1022 AM EST, a mesonet station at the Block Island Jetty recorded a wind gust to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level short wave trough combined with an approaching cold front and a warm, humid environment to produce severe thunderstorms and flash flooding across MA and northern RI during the afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Burrillville, the Harrisville Fire Department was responding to water emergency calls.  One foot of street flooding was reported on Main Street in the Harrisville section.  In the Oakland section of Burrillville, a car was stuck in flood waters in Victory Highway, which was flooded and impassable between Oakland School Street and Remington Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England in the evening, followed by strong to damaging wind gusts.  A cold front then moved through late in the evening, accompanied by a fine line of thunderstorms, which also produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Barrington, a large tree was down on Bay Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passed well to the west of southern New England, with strong to damaging southerly winds across our region.  In addition, 2.00 to 3.75 inches of rain fell across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CWOP station in New Shoreham recorded a wind gust to 58 mph at 136 PM EST and then a gust to 66 mph at 236 PM EST.  At 256 PM EST, the AWOS at Block Island Airport (KBID) recorded a gust to 54 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure passed well to the west of southern New England, with strong to damaging southerly winds across our region.  In addition, 2.00 to 3.75 inches of rain fell across most of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Warwick at 251 PM EST, the ASOS at T. F. Green Airport (KPVD) recorded a wind gust to 58 mph.  Also in Warwick, at 319 PM EST, a tree was down on Hilltop Drive and at 440 PM EST a tree was down on a house on La Rochelle Avenue.

Green Airport (KPVD) in Warwick recorded a gust to 58 mph and at 324 PM EST, an amateur radio operator reported a gust to 61 mph in North Smithfield.  In Pawtucket at 326 PM EST, a tree was down on Seneca Avenue and at 727 PM EST another tree was down on Pleasant Street near Jenks Way.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Johnston at 812 PM EST, a tree was down on wires on Rome Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England in the evening, followed by strong to damaging wind gusts.  A cold front then moved through late in the evening, accompanied by a fine line of thunderstorms, which also produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Middletown at 850 PM EST, a tree was down on Third Beach Road.  At 1033 PM EST, the ASOS at the Newport Airport recorded a gust to 51 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England in the evening, followed by strong to damaging wind gusts.  A cold front then moved through late in the evening, accompanied by a fine line of thunderstorms, which also produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At New Shoreham (Block Island) at 901 PM EST, a mesonet site recorded a wind gust to 69 mph.  The same site recorded a gust to 68 mph at 936 PM EST and 62 mph at 1016 PM EST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England in the evening, followed by strong to damaging wind gusts.  A cold front then moved through late in the evening, accompanied by a fine line of thunderstorms, which also produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Charlestown, multiple trees were down on Klondike Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Scituate, a tree was down on Pine Hill Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: In South Kingstown, Route 138 was closed due to downed trees and wires.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 1045 PM EST, a wind gust to 59 mph was measured in Charlestown.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 62 mph was measured at Conimicut Light.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front moved through southern New England in the evening, followed by strong to damaging wind gusts.  A cold front then moved through late in the evening, accompanied by a fine line of thunderstorms, which also produced some damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Westerly at 1038 PM EST, a wind gust to 68 mph was measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers moved through in the evening hours, accompanied by a modest southerly low-level jet.  Intensifying surface low pressure near the south coast of New England enhanced low level convergence.  These gusty showers knocked down some trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Coventry, trees and wires were down on Whaley Hollow Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers moved through in the evening hours, accompanied by a modest southerly low-level jet.  Intensifying surface low pressure near the south coast of New England enhanced low level convergence.  These gusty showers knocked down some trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Richmond, a tree was down on Gardiner Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of heavy showers moved through in the evening hours, accompanied by a modest southerly low-level jet.  Intensifying surface low pressure near the south coast of New England enhanced low level convergence.  These gusty showers knocked down some trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Johnston, a tree was down on Central Avenue at Reservoir Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Isaias moved from coastal Virginia eastern New York state, near the Connecticut/Massachusetts border on Tuesday, August 4.  As the storm reached the New York City area, southerly winds were maximizing across southern New England, causing widespread wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Westerly, RI at 355 PM EDT, the ASOS at Westerly (KWST) recorded a gust to 51 mph (44 kt).  In North Kingstown at 425 PM EDT, the AWOS Station at Quonset Airport (KOQU) recorded a wind gust to 61 mph.  At 419 PM EDT, a mesonet site at Point Judith recorded a wind gust to 56 mph (49 kt).  Although there were no damage reports from Washington County, it is likely that there was some widely scattered tree or power line damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms formed during the evening hours, out ahead of a strong cold front which swept across southern New England at night.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Newport, about 1 mile south of Rose Island, there was a report forwarded by amateur radio operators, of a person on a sailboat that recorded a wind gust to 75 mph on the boat's anemometer.  Do not know the brand of the equipment.  According to an article on newportri.com/news, two boats sank in the harbor - one was an Alerion Express 28.
